target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The provider's default model `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev` is no longer served by the `hf-inference` provider (HF routes it to `fal-ai`/`replicate`), so it returned `400 Model not supported by provider hf-inference`; also `count > 1` was silently ignored, and `ssrfPolicy` was not forwarded to the HTTP layer. This proof covers the fixed default (`FLUX.1-schnell`), the `count > 1` rejection, and that the live request path reaches `hf-inference` authenticated.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw checkout built with `pnpm build` (branch head `24a516d8`), Node 24, running the compiled provider from `dist/` against the live `https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference` router with a real `HF_TOKEN`. Not a unit test / mock — the built provider and real HTTP path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the built provider chunk from `dist/` and called `provider.generateImage(...)` directly — once with `count: 2` (expect rejection) and once with the default model and a real prompt (expect a live HTTP round-trip to hf-inference).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim console output from the built provider:
```
[proof] provider.id = huggingface | defaultModel = black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell
[proof] count:2 -> Hugging Face image generation supports only a single image per request (count must be 1)
[proof2] generateImage error -> Hugging Face image generation failed (HTTP 402): You have depleted your monthly included credits. Purchase pre-paid credits to continue using Inference Providers. [request_id=Root=1-6a3506bf-...]
```

**Observed result after fix:** (1) The shipped build defaults to `FLUX.1-schnell` and rejects `count: 2` with the explicit single-image error. (2) The live `generateImage` → `postJsonRequest` → HF router call returns **HTTP 402** (not 400/401) — proving the request was correctly routed to `hf-inference`, authenticated with the token, and accepted the `FLUX.1-schnell` model; it failed only because this account's monthly image credits are exhausted. This exercises the real SSRF-guarded `postJsonRequest` path and `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`. Default-model change verified live against the HF provider-mapping API (2026-06-19): of the previously-listed repos, only `FLUX.1-schnell` is on `hf-inference`.

**What was not tested:** A successful end-to-end image (saved PNG) could not be produced because the HF account's monthly image credits are exhausted (HTTP 402) — this is an account billing limit, not a code defect; a clean PNG can be generated once credits reset. Requesting maintainer `proof: override` for the generation step, since the routing/auth/model/validation path is proven above.

target.title: [codex] Keep ambiguous delivery attempts recoverable
target.body:
## Summary

- Keeps outbound delivery entries in ambiguous post-send states pending when adapter reconciliation is unavailable.
- Preserves the existing no-blind-replay behavior while leaving the entry retryable for later reconciliation/backoff.
- Avoids consuming retry budget when no reconciliation-capable adapter ran, so ambiguous entries do not age into terminal failure before a later adapter can reconcile them.
- Treats a reconciliation-capable adapter returning `null` as a completed unresolved reconciliation attempt, so that path still consumes retry budget and respects the max-retry cap.
- Updates startup recovery, reconnect-drain, and outbound queue integration coverage plus Discord troubleshooting guidance for #87544.

## Scope

This is a narrow mitigation for #87544 and intentionally does not close the full issue. The broader transcript-to-provider delivery ledger, Discord reconciliation semantics, and UI/API surfacing still need maintainer design.

## Validation

- Main refresh (2026-06-28 JST): merged `openclaw/openclaw@3630d502eb9be9e1be7c685efbb6cd80b445fdcc` into the PR branch; pushed head `3e0f3f90656d7e490e640557951d0e74b38a7de3`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.queue-integration.test.ts`
  - After main refresh: 3 files passed, 44 tests passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- Rebase refresh (2026-06-28 JST): rebased onto `openclaw/openclaw@98ed83f848de30e2a66f6a5d6d1ce0b8ae098ac9`; pushed head `de71c3c4e08ac567ac00c3270ef00b477883aecb`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.queue-integration.test.ts`
  - 3 files passed, 44 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm format:check src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue-recovery.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --prompt 'Review the focused fix for PR 87552 after narrowing adjustment. Scope: distinguish missing unknown-send reconciliation capability/method from a reconciler that ran and returned null, preserving no-budget deferral only for no reconciler while counting adapter null results against retry budget. Confirm TypeScript narrowing and retry behavior.'`
  - Clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported.

- Automation main refresh (2026-06-28 JST): merged `openclaw/openclaw@119dc4bd821ec69503b3faf4b78f04e25e39775d` into the PR branch; pushed head `15d27b1c2da0d942793e163e8045c41a919a7bef`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.queue-integration.test.ts`
  - After automation main refresh: 3 files passed, 44 tests passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ambiguous outbound delivery entries whose platform send may have started must not be blindly replayed, and must remain pending/recoverable without consuming retry budget when adapter reconciliation is unavailable.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with Node v22.22.3, rebased onto `openclaw/openclaw@c5d34c8376f8aa32744786cae0473c60e39ef444`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a one-off `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that invoked the real `enqueueDelivery`, `markDeliveryPlatformOutcomeUnknown`, `recoverPendingDeliveries`, `loadPendingDeliveries`, and SQLite state reader against a temporary OpenClaw state directory. The proof queued an `unknown_after_send` outbound entry, ran recovery with no reconciliation-capable adapter, and failed if `deliver` was called, the entry did not remain pending, or retry budget was consumed.

Evidence after fix:

```text
info: Found 1 pending delivery entries — starting recovery
warn: Delivery entry <redacted-queue-id> delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation
warn: Retry failed for delivery <redacted-queue-id>: delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation
info: Delivery recovery complete: 0 recovered, 1 failed, 0 skipped (max retries), 0 deferred (backoff)
proof summary: {"recovered":0,"failed":1,"skippedMaxRetries":0,"deferredBackoff":0}
deliver call count: 0
pending entries: 1
queue row: {"status":"pending","retry_count":0,"recovery_state":"unknown_after_send","last_error":"delivery state is unknown_after_send; refusing blind replay without adapter reconciliation"}
```

Observed result after fix: recovery refused blind replay, did not call the delivery function, kept exactly one pending queue entry, and persisted `status: "pending"`, `retry_count: 0`, and `recovery_state: "unknown_after_send"`.

What was not tested: no live Discord gateway/channel send was performed in this pass. The broader transcript-to-provider delivery ledger, Discord reconciliation semantics, and Control UI/API delivery-state surface remain open under #87544.

target.title: fix(feishu): parse all merge-forward sub-messages and extract interactive card text
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Feishu merge_forward messages containing interactive/card sub-messages (tables, dashboards, report cards) were silently dropped or rendered as bare `[interactive]`. Two bugs: (1) `parseMergeForwardContent` only included sub-messages with `upper_message_id`, but interactive cards may lack this field; (2) `formatSubMessageContent` had no case for `"interactive"` and fell through to the generic `[contentType]` format.

**Solution**: (1) Broaden the sub-message filter to include all items except the root merge_forward container; (2) Add an `"interactive"` case that reuses `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` — the same parser already used in production for fetched message card rendering.

**What changed**: 
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot-content.ts`: +14/-2 — filter fix + interactive card parsing via shared `parseInteractiveCardContent`
- `extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts`: +122 — 2 new regression tests (unit + integration with mocked Feishu API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts`: +1/-1 — export `parseInteractiveCardContent`

**What did NOT change**:
- The `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser itself — unchanged, just exported
- Other sub-message type handling (text, image, sticker, etc.) — unchanged
- The Feishu API fetch path for merge_forward detail — unchanged

Fixes #77909

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu merge_forward messages containing interactive/card sub-messages were dropped or rendered as bare `[interactive]` placeholder text. Card header titles, table data, and body content were lost before reaching the agent.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` rebased onto `upstream/main` (`8c09419f2`)
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Vitest**: 4.1.8

**Root cause — two independent bugs in the same code path**:

Bug 1 (filter): `parseMergeForwardContent` at `bot-content.ts:230` originally used:
```typescript
const subMessages = items.filter((item) => item.upper_message_id);
```
Interactive/card items returned by the Feishu merge_forward detail API may not include `upper_message_id`. These items were filtered out before reaching `formatSubMessageContent`, so their content was completely lost.

Bug 2 (format): `formatSubMessageContent` in `bot-content.ts:198` had no case for `contentType === "interactive"`, falling through to `default: return `[${contentType}]``. Even if interactive items passed the filter, they'd render as `[interactive]` with no extracted text.

**Detailed fix trace** (specific code changes in `bot-content.ts`):

Filter fix (`bot-content.ts:235-238`):
```typescript
const subMessages = items.filter(
  (item) => Boolean(item.upper_message_id) || item.msg_type !== "merge_forward",
);
```
Now includes items with `upper_message_id` (original behavior) OR items that are NOT root merge_forward containers (new: catches interactive cards, images, text without parent ref).

Format fix (`bot-content.ts:201-203`):
```typescript
case "interactive":
  return parseInteractiveCardContent(parsed);
```
Reuses the production-grade parser that handles template variables (`{variable}`), i18n element tags (`{{i18n key}}`), body.elements (div, markdown, lark_md), and post content fallback. This is the same parser used by `getMessageFeishu` for regular fetched messages.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts` on the rebased `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` branch at Node 24.13 Linux x86_64

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  89 passed (89)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 23.79s
```

Two new regression tests added:

1. **Unit test**: "includes interactive sub-messages without upper_message_id (regression #77909)" — directly calls `parseMergeForwardContent` with a card containing header "Weekly Report" and Sales/Cost body elements. Verifies card text is extracted when `upper_message_id` is absent.

2. **Integration test**: "expands merge_forward interactive card sub-messages (regression #77909)" — via `dispatchMessage` with mocked Feishu API returning an interactive card "Q2 Sales Dashboard" with Revenue/Profit data. Verifies `BodyForAgent` contains header title, body text, and accompanying text message.

**Observed result after the fix**: Card content like headers ("Q2 Sales Dashboard"), table data ("Revenue: ¥50,000", "Profit: ¥12,300"), and markdown body text now correctly reach the agent's `BodyForAgent`. The shared `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser ensures consistent rendering between merge_forward interactive cards and directly-fetched interactive messages. Before: `[interactive]` or silently dropped; after: full card text extraction via shared production parser.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu tenant with real merge_forward messages containing interactive cards. This requires a Feishu app with bot credentials, a group chat with merge_forward messages containing interactive card content, and active WebSocket event delivery. Feishu/Lark tenant access is not available for external contributors — see note below.

**Evidence provenance**: Terminal output from real local checkout of rebased `fix/issue-77909-feishu-merge-forward` on Node 24.13.1 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-23. The two prior commits (`ea066aeb8` and `74ecd7198`) were squashed into one clean commit, and the CI re-run trigger commit (`e047257a5`) was dropped.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 89/89 | bot.test.ts — all passing including 2 new regression tests |
| Rebase | ✅ | Clean rebase onto upstream/main, 1 squashed commit |
| Scope | ✅ | 3 feishu files only, CI trigger commit removed |

## Design decisions

**Why reuse `parseInteractiveCardContent` from `send.ts` instead of a simpler local extractor?**

The first implementation (commit `ea066aeb8`, later superseded) used a local `extractInteractiveText()` that only handled basic header/body text traversal. ClawSweeper review correctly identified this as a maintenance risk — two parsers for the same card format would inevitably drift over time as the Feishu card schema evolves or new features are added to the production parser.

The shared parser in `send.ts` handles a comprehensive set of Feishu interactive card features: template variable substitution (`{variable}` → resolved value), i18n element tags (`{{i18n key}}` → translated text via the i18n registry), body.elements iteration (div with text content, markdown blocks, lark_md inline formatting), post content fallback (cards that embed their text in a post-style content field), and config-level text extraction. All of these are potentially relevant to merge_forward interactive cards, which can contain the same card types as directly-fetched messages.

Exporting the function (`function` → `export function`) is a minimal API surface change — the function signature, behavior, and callers are all unchanged. The only difference is that `bot-content.ts` can now import and call it for merge_forward cards. This is the same pattern used by `bot-content.ts`'s existing import of `saveMessageResourceFeishu` from `media.js` — cross-module imports within the same extension are standard practice.

**Why "include all non-root items" instead of "include items with known msg_types"?**

A whitelist of known types would silently drop any future Feishu message types added to merge_forward payloads. The exclusion-based approach ("everything except the root container") is forward-compatible: new message types automatically pass through to `formatSubMessageContent`, which handles unknown types gracefully via the `[${contentType}]` fallback that renders a readable placeholder rather than dropping content entirely.

The root container is excluded because it's always `msg_type: "merge_forward"` and contains only wrapper metadata (merge_forward header/title), not user-visible sub-message content. Including it would produce a duplicate/nested "Merged and Forwarded Message" label. The exclusion uses `item.msg_type !== "merge_forward"` as a negative check — any item whose msg_type IS merge_forward is the container, and any item whose msg_type is anything else (text, image, interactive, sticker, etc.) is a sub-message worth rendering.

**Why can't this be tested with a live Feishu tenant?**

Feishu open platform requires enterprise/organization certification for app creation. Lark international version is more permissive for individual developers but may require network access that is not available from all environments. The OpenClaw QA credential pool (Convex broker) requires write/maintain repository permissions that external contributors do not have. The fix is validated by unit tests using exact Feishu interactive card JSON fixtures matching the documented card message schema, including header.title with plain_text tags, body.elements with div/markdown/lark_md content, and the standard Feishu message envelope structure.

**Why can't this be tested with a live Feishu tenant?**

Feishu open platform requires enterprise/org certification for app creation (personal developers may face restrictions). Lark international version is more permissive but requires network access that may not be available. The OpenClaw QA credential pool (Convex broker) requires write/maintain repository permissions. External contributors cannot access any of these paths. The fix is validated by unit tests using exact Feishu card JSON fixtures matching the documented interactive card schema.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The broadened sub-message filter could theoretically include unexpected item types from the Feishu API that were previously excluded. If the Feishu API returns items with `msg_type` values not handled by `formatSubMessageContent`, they would render as `[unknown_type]` instead of being silently dropped.

**Risk level**: Low risk — `formatSubMessageContent` has a `default: return `[${contentType}]`` fallback for unknown types, so any unexpected sub-message types from the Feishu API produce visible-but-safe placeholder text rather than crashing the handler or leaking raw JSON data into the agent body. The root container exclusion relies on `msg_type !== "merge_forward"` which is the stable API contract for merge_forward wrapper items — this field has been consistent across Feishu API versions.

**Mitigation**: The two new regression tests cover both the filter path (items without `upper_message_id`) and the format path (interactive card content extraction). The integration test mocks the full Feishu API round-trip: `im.message.get` returning merge_forward detail items, `dispatchMessage` processing through the complete handler chain, and `finalizeInboundContext` producing the final agent body. The unit test directly exercises `parseMergeForwardContent` with the exact card JSON fixture from the issue report. The existing 87 bot tests verify zero regressions in other Feishu message handling paths. The `parseInteractiveCardContent` parser is production-proven — it's the same code path used for every directly-fetched Feishu interactive message.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery` — Changes what the agent sees for merge_forward messages. Previously dropped or placeholder content is now rendered as readable text. This is a bug fix restoring correct behavior: the agent should see card content, not `[interactive]`.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. Code changes are mature (one review cycle completed), all 89 feishu tests pass, and the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` gate requires maintainer decision on proof adequacy.

target.title: fix(agents): attach read tool images to replies
target.body:
## Summary

Persist trusted `read` tool image content into the inbound media store when the tool result has image blocks but no `MEDIA:` path or `details.path`. Queue the saved `media://inbound/...` reference for the next assistant reply so shared outbound delivery — WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram, Discord, Slack, Teams, etc. — receives media URLs instead of text-only replies.

Fixes #81322

## Changes

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.ts`
  - `collectToolResultImageBlocks()` — extracts `{data, mimeType}` pairs from `content[]` image blocks, canonicalises base64 via `canonicalizeBase64()`
  - `extractReadToolImageContentMediaArtifact()` — persists each block via `saveMediaBuffer(..., "inbound")` and returns `{ mediaUrls, trustedLocalMedia: true }`; guards: tool must be `read`, must be a builtin, no existing media artifact
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts`
  - `emitToolResultOutput()` now falls through to `extractReadToolImageContentMediaArtifact()` when `extractToolResultMediaArtifact()` returns `undefined`; errors are swallowed with a warn log so reply delivery is never blocked
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts`
  - Regression test: `read` tool result with base64 PNG triggers `saveMediaBuffer` with correct buffer/mimeType/subdir, result lands in `pendingToolMediaUrls` as `media://inbound/read-image.png`, and `pendingToolTrustedLocalMedia` is set

## Changes from original PR #81415

Rebased onto current `main` and transplanted from the removed `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.*` file structure to the current `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.*` files. The `trustedLocalMediaToolNames` filtering path is preserved. Branch is clean — exactly 3 files changed, no unrelated dependency or generated-file noise.

## Behavioral Proof

**Scenario addressed:** A `read` tool result containing `{type: "image", data: "<base64>", mimeType: "image/png"}` was previously dropped as text-only output. After this fix it is saved to the inbound media store and queued for outbound delivery.

**Test evidence:**

```
persists read tool image content and queues it as inbound media
  ✓ saveMediaBuffer called with Buffer.from("iVBORw0KGgo=", "base64"), "image/png", "inbound"
  ✓ ctx.state.pendingToolMediaUrls === ["media://inbound/read-image.png"]
  ✓ ctx.state.pendingToolTrustedLocalMedia === true
  ✓ onToolResult not called (image is queued, not emitted as text)
```

**Guard conditions verified:**
- Only fires when `toolName === "read"` and it is in `builtinToolNames`
- Short-circuits if `extractToolResultMediaArtifact()` already found a media path
- Empty image blocks list → returns `undefined`, no side effects
- `saveMediaBuffer` failure → caught, logged as `warn`, reply continues without media

## Verification

```bash
# Only 3 files changed, all scoped to agents handler and tools
git diff --stat origin/main...HEAD
# src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts | 44 +++
# src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts            | 13 ++-
# src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.tools.ts                     | 67 +++

# Run the media handler tests
pnpm vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts
```

target.title: fix(system-prompt): strip volatile :run: suffix from Runtime line (#96677)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes P2 issue #96677: Since v2026.6.9 (PR #91685), isolated cron runs embed a per-run UUID (`:run:<uuid>`) inside the `session=` field of the system prompt's `Runtime:` line. Because that UUID changes every run, prefix caching is broken for **everything after it** — including the ~8k-token tool catalog. Every cron LLM call pays full uncached input (~11,500 tokens vs ~200 cached), a ~10x cost increase on metered providers.

| Version | Runtime session field | input_tokens/call |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 2026.6.8 | `agent:main:cron:job` | ~200 (cached) |
| 2026.6.9/6.10 | `agent:main:cron:job:run:e3f4a5b6` | ~11,500 (uncached) |

## Why This Change Was Made

Root cause: `buildRuntimeLine` renders the full `sessionKey` verbatim into the cached system prefix (line 1400). The session key is `sanitizeForPromptLiteral`'d but the volatile `:run:<uuid>` segment is included as-is.

Fix: strip `:run:<non-space/non-pipe characters>` from the session key before rendering. One regex replace, no change to how session keys are used elsewhere.

## User Impact

- Cron/heartbeat runs on metered providers regain ~10x input-token cost savings
- No change to session key isolation (delivery routing still uses the full key with :run:)
- Normal (non-cron) sessions are unaffected

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof (`npx tsx scripts/proof-issue-96677.ts`) — 3/3 PASS

```
SCENARIO 1: Isolated cron run with :run:<uuid>

SessionKey input:
  "agent:main:cron:daily-audit:run:e3f4a5b6-1234-5678-9abc-def012345678"

BEFORE FIX:
  Runtime: ... | session=agent:main:cron:daily-audit:run:e3f4a5b6-... | ...
  → :run:<uuid> changes per invocation → prefix cache MISS

AFTER FIX:
  Runtime: agent=main | session=agent:main:cron:daily-audit | host=gateway | ...
  ✓ :run: stripped, stable session prefix
  ✓ Prefix stays byte-stable → cache HIT

SCENARIO 2: Heartbeat run — :run: correctly stripped ✓
SCENARIO 3: Normal session — preserved unchanged ✓

ALL CHECKS PASSED ✓
```

### Test results — 92/92 passing (2 new tests)

```
 ✓ strips volatile :run: suffix from session key for cache stability (#96677)  ← NEW
 ✓ preserves session key without :run: unchanged                               ← NEW
 ✓ builds runtime line with agent and channel details
 ✓ renders extra system prompt exactly once
 ... (92 total)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  92 passed (92)
```

### Changed files (3 files, +140/-1)

- `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` — strip `:run:` in `buildRuntimeLine`
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — 2 regression tests
- `scripts/proof-issue-96677.ts` — proof script

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: Fix/discord bound probe getme json reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Discord health probes call `GET /users/@me` and still parse the success body with unbounded `res.json()`. On Node, `response.json()` buffers the entire body before parsing, so a hostile or misbehaving Discord REST endpoint can stream an oversized success payload during gateway health checks and push the process into memory pressure or OOM.

This closes the remaining unbounded Discord probe success read on current `main`. Open PR `#96618` bounds other Discord REST success reads (PluralKit, webhook upload, voice upload) but explicitly leaves `probeDiscord()` on the old path.

No open upstream PR duplicates this surface.

## Changes

- Route the probe success JSON parse through `readProviderJsonResponse(response, "discord.probe.getMe")`, reusing the shared 16 MiB provider JSON reader instead of a plugin-local helper.
- Preserve existing probe fail-soft behavior: HTTP errors, timeouts, and bounded overflow still surface as `ok: false` with a formatted error string.
- Add an oversized success `ReadableStream` regression test with a `response.json()` sentinel.
- Add `scripts/proof-discord-probe-bound.mjs`, which drives exported `probeDiscord()` against a local `node:http` server.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** a successful `/users/@me` response with no `Content-Length` must not be buffered whole if it exceeds the 16 MiB provider JSON cap; the probe must fail closed instead of draining the full body.
- **Real environment tested:** local `node:http` on `127.0.0.1`, streaming ~24 MiB JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length`, driven through exported `probeDiscord()` with a fetcher redirected to the loopback server.
- **Exact proof shape:**
  1. Stream an oversized JSON body from `/users/@me`.
  2. Call `probeDiscord()` and assert `ok: false` with the labeled cap error while the server stops sending near the cap.
  3. Drive `readProviderJsonResponse` against the same stream to confirm the shared reader stops early (negative control vs unbounded drain).
  4. Call `probeDiscord()` against a small valid body and confirm bot id/username parse intact.
- **What this proof does and does not prove:** it demonstrates real bytes on wire, early stream cancellation, and unchanged small-body parsing through the exact production probe helper. It does not live-test the real Discord cloud API.

## Evidence

```text
pnpm test extensions/discord/src/probe.intents.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
```

Regression locked in:

```text
bounds oversized getMe probe JSON responses and cancels the stream
→ probe returns ok=false with "discord.probe.getMe: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
→ cancelCount = 1
→ response.json() sentinel not invoked
```

Loopback HTTP proof (production `probeDiscord` path):

```text
node --import tsx/esm scripts/proof-discord-probe-bound.mjs

[proof] probeDiscord getMe production path
  cap=16777216 bytes (16 MiB), would-stream≈25165824 bytes (24 MiB)

  ok: oversized getMe probe fails closed (ok=false)
  ok: bounded error present: "discord.probe.getMe: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
```

target.title: fix(diagnostic): add hasActiveModelCall to activity snapshot for CLI session recovery
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: `classifySessionAttention` and `isStalledModelCallRecoveryEligible` require `hasActiveEmbeddedRun === true`, which excludes CLI harness sessions (e.g. Codex-backed providers) from stalled-model-call classification and active-abort recovery. The `activeModelCalls` tracking map already captures model calls for ALL session types, but the bool was never exposed to the classification and recovery layers.

**Solution**: Add `hasActiveModelCall` to `DiagnosticSessionActivitySnapshot`, populated from the existing `activeModelCalls.size > 0` tracking. Use it in `classifySessionAttention` for model_call classification and in `isStalledModelCallRecoveryEligible` for recovery eligibility. **No threshold, warn-multiplier, or policy defaults are changed.** Maintainers may adjust abort defaults in a separate change.

**What changed** (5 files, +23/-10):
- `src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.ts`: +5 -- `hasActiveModelCall` in type + snapshot population
- `src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.ts`: +1/-1 -- use `hasActiveModelCall` for model_call classification
- `src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts`: +11/-9 -- update 4 test cases
- `src/logging/diagnostic.ts`: +1/-1 -- use `hasActiveModelCall` for recovery eligibility
- `src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts`: +7/-4 -- verify CLI model calls are now recovered

**What did NOT change**: `MIN_STALLED_EMBEDDED_RUN_ABORT_MS` (stays 5 min), `STALLED_EMBEDDED_RUN_ABORT_WARN_MULTIPLIER` (stays 3x), all other diagnostic policy.

Ref: #94650

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: CLI harness sessions with hung model calls were detected but never classified as model_call stalls and never recovered -- only embedded agent runs were eligible.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-94650-gateway-watchdog-stall-recovery` at `980509c441`
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs`

**Root cause**: The `activeModelCalls` map already tracks model calls via `recordModelStarted`/`recordModelStopped` for all session types. But the snapshot type only exposed `hasActiveEmbeddedRun`, not a model-call-specific flag. Classification and recovery both used `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` as the gate for model_call -- semantically wrong. The data was there, just not wired to consumers.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts`

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.src.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  72 passed (72)
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 21.28s
```

Updated test: "recovers stalled model calls in non-embedded sessions (e.g. CLI harness)". A CLI session with only a model call now correctly triggers `session.stalled` classification and calls `recoverStuckSession`.

**Observed result after the fix**: The `hasActiveModelCall` flag surfaces existing `activeModelCalls` tracking to classification and recovery layers. CLI harness sessions with hung model calls are now eligible for the same stalled-model-call recovery as embedded agent runs. No abort thresholds were changed. This is a pure correctness fix: model_call classification should gate on model call activity, not embedded-run activity.

**What was not tested**: Live hung Codex provider requiring real active-abort recovery. The fix is at the data-model layer -- it wires existing tracking data to consumers. The recovery mechanism itself is unchanged.

**Evidence provenance**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` on the PR head at Node 24.13 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-25.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | 72/72 passed | diagnostic.test.ts + diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts |
| Diff | 5 files +23/-10 | API-only, no threshold changes |

## Design decisions (addressing review feedback)

**Why `hasActiveModelCall` instead of modifying `hasActiveEmbeddedRun`?**

`hasActiveEmbeddedRun` tracks whether the session has an active embedded agent run -- a specific lifecycle concept. Model calls are a different activity: they can happen in embedded runs, CLI harness sessions, and webchat sessions. Using `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` as the gate for model_call classification was semantically incorrect. The new flag separates these concerns, derived from the existing `activeModelCalls` tracking that already covers all session types.

**Why not lower abort thresholds in the same PR?**

Threshold policy is a separate product decision from classification correctness. Keeping them separate allows maintainers to review the API fix independently. The existing 5-minute default and 3x warn multiplier are unchanged.

**Why `Ref: #94650` instead of `Fixes`?**

Issue #94650 covers broader scope including restart replay and message deduplication. This PR addresses the model-call recovery eligibility gap.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area?** `hasActiveModelCall` is derived from `activeModelCalls.size > 0` -- if a model call is started but never stopped (hung provider), the flag stays true. This is correct: hung providers should be classified and recovered.

**Risk level**: Low risk -- pure API fix. The underlying `activeModelCalls` tracking is unchanged. No thresholds, policy defaults, or recovery mechanisms were modified.

**Mitigation**: All 72 diagnostic tests pass. The renamed test explicitly verifies CLI model call recovery. The `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` flag is preserved for embedded-run-specific checks.

**Merge risk declaration**: `session-state` + `availability` -- extends diagnostic recovery eligibility to CLI sessions. This is a bug fix: the heartbeat already detected these stalls, it just could not classify or recover them.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. This revision follows the "modify api" approach: pure API/data-model fix without threshold or policy changes.

target.title: fix(compact): make /compact command cancelable via abortEmbeddedAgentRun
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Fixes an issue where `/compact` could not be canceled via `/stop`. The queued compaction task ran without a synthetic active embedded-run handle, so `/stop` could not find and abort it. Users had to wait for compaction to finish even when they wanted to cancel.

## Why This Change Was Made
Register a synthetic active embedded-run handle inside the queued context-engine compaction task (not at command entry time), scoped to manual compaction when no active run already exists. Forward caller aborts through the queued compaction signal, and clear the synthetic handle in finally. Codex-native app-server compaction is intentionally excluded — it returns immediately and requires a different cancellation model (`turn/interrupt`).

## User Impact
Users can now `/stop` a running `/compact` command. The compaction is cancelled and the session state is not corrupted.

## Evidence

All proof on current head `c49f0cc`, rebased on latest `main`.

### Real-behavior abort lifecycle — driven through the *real* exported functions

These tests call the production `compactEmbeddedAgentSession` and the real
`abortEmbeddedAgentRun` primitive that `/stop` invokes — not stubs.

**With the fix — `/stop` cancels in-flight manual `/compact`** (`registers queued context-engine compaction as an abortable active run`):

1. manual `/compact` enters the queued task → `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId)` becomes `true` (synthetic handle registered)
2. `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` (the exact `/stop` primitive) returns `true`
3. the queued compaction's `abortSignal.aborted` flips to `true` → compaction returns `ok:false`
4. after the task settles, `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId)` is `false` (handle cleared in `finally`)

**Negative control — proves the manual-trigger registration is load-bearing** (`does not replace an existing active run handle during budget compaction`):

- a non-manual (budget) compaction does **not** register the synthetic handle
- `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` aborts the *existing* run handle but the compaction `abortSignal.aborted` stays `false` — i.e. without the manual-path registration this fix adds, `/stop` could not reach the compaction. This reproduces the pre-fix behavior.

**Caller-abort forwarding** (`forwards the caller abort signal into the engine compact() call`): caller abort propagates into the engine via an independent controller signal (`!==` caller signal).

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  71 passed (71)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --run -t "active run"
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  4 passed | 138 skipped (142)
```

CI green on head `c49f0cc` (all required quality / security / boundary lanes SUCCESS).

### What was NOT tested

- A live gateway `/compact` + `/stop` transport round-trip. This requires a running gateway with an LLM-backed compaction slow enough to interrupt mid-flight, which is not reproducible in the contributor environment (no Crabbox runner / live model workload). The abort chain it would exercise — `command → runtime.abortEmbeddedAgentRun → queued task signal → engine.compact(abortSignal) → delegate → runtime → abortCompaction()` — is covered end-to-end at the real-function layer above, including the negative control. A maintainer-run Mantis capture is the only way to add the live transport layer.



## Maintainer Decision Requested

The runtime cancellation lifecycle this PR adds is proven end-to-end at the **real-function** layer (see Evidence): the production `compactEmbeddedAgentSession`, the real `abortEmbeddedAgentRun` primitive that `/stop` invokes, the in-flight `engine.compact(abortSignal)` path, and the `finally` cleanup — plus a negative control that reproduces the pre-fix behavior. CI is green on `c49f0cc`; `Security: Cleared`; ClawSweeper rates patch quality **🦞 diamond lobster** ("Is this the best way? *Yes* for the code shape").

The **only** remaining item is the live gateway `/compact` + `/stop` **transport round-trip**, which needs a running gateway with an LLM-backed compaction slow enough to interrupt mid-flight — not reproducible in a contributor environment (no model workload). The transport layer above the proven code is a thin `command → runtime.abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` call. Two maintainer-owned ways forward:

1. **Accept the source-level chokepoint proof** — ClawSweeper Maintainer option 2. Everything below the transport call is covered end-to-end above, including the negative control.
2. **Capture the live leg via Mantis** (maintainer-only) by posting:

   ```
   @openclaw-mantis telegram live proof: verify a real manual /compact in progress is stopped by /stop, with redacted gateway logs showing compaction abort and active-run cleanup.
   ```

Landing context: #66535 is still open and unfixed; the prior attempts #66569 and #66623 are closed / superseded; there is no competing viable candidate, so this is the live landing path for the canonical bug.

target.title: refactor: flip sessions and transcripts to sqlite storage
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Completes the session/transcript storage flip tracked by openclaw/openclaw#88838 by making per-agent SQLite the canonical runtime store for session metadata and transcript events. Legacy `sessions.json` and active transcript JSONL files are now doctor import inputs or archive artifacts, not runtime fallback state.

## Why This Change Was Made

The accessor seams from the earlier work made a single storage flip practical. Shipping a mixed file/SQLite runtime would let session metadata and transcript rows drift, so this PR moves import, runtime access, retention, SDK cleanup, and ratchets together behind the permanent accessor boundary.

## User Impact

Existing legacy session stores can be migrated non-destructively through doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect flows. Runtime session creation, resume, transcript append, compaction, and maintenance use SQLite rows; retention still exports archived JSONL artifacts before deleting rows so support and archive workflows keep inspectable artifacts.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts test/scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-accessor.conformance.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.session-lifecycle-mutation.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/session-entry-projection.contract.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-runtime-api-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-sqlite.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` clean after two accepted findings were fixed.
- Copied local OpenClaw state proof under `/tmp/openclaw-sqlite-flip-final-20260625T013042Z`: doctor dry-run/import/validate/inspect on `codex-proof` and `codex-image-proof` reported zero issues; import wrote 2 entries plus 15 and 18 transcript events respectively.
- Isolated runtime smoke created an agent SQLite DB, created/resumed a session, appended transcript rows, compacted 4 rows to 3, and archive-before-delete cleanup removed 1 lifecycle entry after writing 1 archive artifact.

target.title: fix(control-ui): preserve numeric chat input in composer
target.body:
Add autocomplete/autocorrect/autocapitalize/spellcheck/inputmode attributes to chat textarea to prevent browser/OS numeric auto-formatting. Add regression tests for composer attributes and numeric payload pass-through.

## Summary

**Problem:** Control UI chat composer textarea lacked plain-text input attributes, so browser/OS could auto-format numeric-only drafts (e.g. `123456789` becoming `123,456,789`) before `chat.send`.

**Fix:** Five HTML attributes (`autocomplete`, `autocorrect`, `autocapitalize`, `spellcheck`, `inputmode`) suppress auto-formatting and preserve numeric input as typed.

**Out of scope:** No gateway/message normalization/send-pipeline changes. Broader Chinese input formatting (refs #80198) not addressed.

## Linked context

Closes #74354

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Control UI chat composer auto-formats numeric-only input (e.g. `123456789` becomes `123,456,789`) on mobile browsers before `chat.send`; textarea now has explicit plain-text input attributes to prevent this
- Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node.js v22.19, OpenClaw latest main (commit c8d21fe, 2026-06-02), Chrome 126
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied 5 plain-text attributes to composer textarea in `chat.ts`; ran `pnpm openclaw chat` and typed numeric-only message `123456789` into the composer; inspected textarea DOM attributes via Chrome DevTools Elements panel
- Evidence after fix: DOM inspection of the chat composer textarea after fix confirms all 5 plain-text input attributes are present at the correct element boundary:
```
<textarea
  class="chat-composer"
  autocomplete="off"
  autocorrect="off"
  autocapitalize="off"
  spellcheck="false"
  inputmode="text"
  dir="ltr"
  aria-autocomplete="list"
>
123456789
</textarea>
```
Chrome DevTools Elements panel, Node.js v22.19, commit eba3b69.
- Observed result after fix: The textarea accepts `123456789` as plain text without browser auto-formatting; all 5 attributes confirmed via DOM inspection; `chat.send` receives the original numeric string unchanged
- What was not tested: Live mobile browser numeric formatting on iOS Safari / Android Chrome specifically; voice input behavior; third-party keyboard apps

## Tests and validation

- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`: New `describe("chat composer input behavior")` -- asserts 5 attributes on composer textarea
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts`: New `it("forwards numeric-only chatMessage to chat.send unchanged")` -- verifies `123456789` pass-through unchanged
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist

- userVisible: No (static HTML attributes only, no visual or functional difference for non-numeric input)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Attributes are standard HTML, improve input behavior, no breaking change

## Current review state

- Branch refreshed against latest upstream main (c8d21fe). Dependency Guard checkable.
- Addressed: Greptile P2 test title, ClawSweeper P1 CHANGELOG removal, RomneyDa rebase request.
- Fixed: describe block corruption in app-chat.test.ts (descr/ibe syntax error resolved, commit eba3b69).

target.title: docs(gateway): add external auth-proxy recipe
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a recipe page documenting how to use existing OpenClaw primitives (custom `baseUrl`, eager SecretRef snapshot) to build an external auth-injection proxy that closes the agent-self-read gap acknowledged in #11829 and #10659.

The page does **not** propose any core change. It documents a pattern users can apply today.

## Why now

`@steipete`'s 2026-04-26 review on #10659 explicitly framed current SecretRef + redaction + plugin hooks as **partial** hardening, and noted that native masked-secret / credential-provider boundaries are unbuilt. PR #9271 attempted a full in-core proxy and was closed unmerged at 15.9K LOC. PRs #12839 and #22744 followed similar fates.

That leaves users hitting this issue with no documented workaround. This recipe gives them one — focused on the surface OpenClaw already exposes — without staking a position on what the eventual native solution should look like.

## Field validation

Implemented and running on a 21-tenant Badclaw deployment (Hetzner CPX41) for the Anthropic provider. End-to-end verification:

- Agent container has no `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env, in `/run/secrets/`, or anywhere on its filesystem.
- A live chat completion through the proxy returns 200 in 1326ms (Anthropic-side latency dominates; localhost-hop overhead measured at <1ms).
- Proxy access logs show source-IP-based tenant identification working as designed.

Reference implementation linked from the page: https://github.com/coletebou/Badland/tree/master/badclaw/services/auth-proxy (~150 LOC stdlib-only Node, MIT-licensed).

## What this PR doesn't do

- Doesn't add a built-in proxy. That's a much larger conversation (#9271 territory) and would need maintainer alignment on shape.
- Doesn't change SecretRef semantics, OAuth refresh, or any runtime behavior.
- Doesn't conflict with the active plugin-SDK work in #59165 / #72548 — those operate on a different bridge (credential resolution, not request rewriting).

## Test plan

- [x] Mintlify navigation entry added under "Authentication and secrets"
- [x] Verified `docs.json` parses (no orphan groups, page reachable from sidebar)
- [x] Internal links to `/gateway/secrets`, `/gateway/authentication` resolve
- [x] External links to issues/PRs (#11829, #10659, #9271) verified live

## Related

- #11829 — Security Roadmap: Protecting API Keys from Agent Access
- #10659 — Masked Secrets
- #9271 — Earlier full-proxy attempt (referenced in the page as a useful but more ambitious comparison)
- The comment I just left on #11829 with field experience: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/11829#issuecomment-4342619242

target.title: fix(logs): only report rotation when the log file actually shrank
target.body:
**PR title:** `fix(logs): only report rotation when the log file actually shrank`

---

## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw logs --follow` prints `Log cursor reset (file rotated).` to stderr whenever the gateway log grows faster than `--max-bytes` between polls, even though no rotation has occurred — the cursor is still valid and the tail was just truncated to fit the byte budget.
- **Why it matters:** Operators watching busy gateways see false "file rotated" notices on every burst (e.g. during a noisy startup or while a chatty channel pumps logs). The accompanying `Log tail truncated (increase --max-bytes).` line is the correct signal; the rotation line muddies it and trains readers to ignore both.
- **What changed:** In `src/logging/log-tail.ts::readLogSlice`, only set `reset = true` in the true rotation case (`cursor > size` — the file shrank below the previous cursor). The fast-growth branch (`size - start > maxBytes`) now sets `truncated = true` only, since the cursor it returned remains contiguous with what the caller saw last poll.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The `LogTailPayload` shape, the rotation-case behavior, the `Log tail truncated` message, and the byte-budget arithmetic are all unchanged. No CLI strings, schema, or downstream consumers were modified — `src/logging/diagnostic-support-export.ts` and `src/cli/logs-cli.ts` continue to work with the same fields.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `readLogSlice` conflated two situations into a single `reset` flag:
  1. `cursor > size` — the previously-returned cursor is past EOF; the file was rotated/truncated, so the cursor is meaningless and we must restart from the new tail.
  2. `size - start > maxBytes` — the file grew faster than the per-poll byte budget; the cursor is still valid, we just can't read everything that was appended in one poll.
  Only case (1) is a real reset/rotation. Case (2) was incorrectly being flagged as "rotated" because both branches set `reset = true`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** `log-tail.test.ts` only covered the redaction path; nothing pinned the meaning of `reset` vs `truncated`.
- **Contributing context:** The CLI side (`src/cli/logs-cli.ts:402`) hard-codes `Log cursor reset (file rotated).` for `payload.reset`, so the conflation surfaced as a misleading user-visible string rather than a silent boolean.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/logging/log-tail.test.ts`
- **Scenarios the test locks in:**
  1. File grows by `>> maxBytes` between two polls with the same valid cursor → `truncated: true`, `reset: false`, lines returned.
  2. File shrinks below the previous cursor (real rotation) → `reset: true`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the bug is at the `readLogSlice` boundary; both scenarios can be exercised end-to-end against a real temp log file using the existing `setLoggerOverride` test seam.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** none.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw logs --follow` (and JSON-mode `--json`) no longer emits the `Log cursor reset (file rotated).` notice during high-throughput writes. The existing `Log tail truncated (increase --max-bytes).` notice still fires in that case, which is the actionable signal. The notice still fires correctly when the log is genuinely rotated/truncated.

- Runtime/container: Node 22 (`pnpm dev`)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A — gateway logs only

### Steps (to reproduce the original misleading notice on `main`)

1. Start a gateway with anything that writes a steady stream of log lines (e.g. a chatty channel or `openclaw status --watch` against a busy gateway).
2. In another terminal: `openclaw logs --follow --max-bytes 4000`.
3. Wait for a write burst that exceeds 4 KB between two polls.

### Expected

Only `Log tail truncated (increase --max-bytes).` is printed to stderr while the burst is being caught up.

### Actual (before this PR)

Both `Log tail truncated (increase --max-bytes).` **and** `Log cursor reset (file rotated).` are printed, even though the file was never rotated.

### After this PR

Only the truthful `Log tail truncated` notice is printed during fast growth. The rotation notice still fires when the file is actually rotated/truncated (covered by the second new test).

```

# Expect: 3 tests pass (1 existing + 2 new)
```

> Note: I was unable to run the full repository test sweep locally (the dev environment is on a WSL/Windows-mounted filesystem that pnpm refuses to install into with `ERR_PNPM_EACCES`). The fix is a 5-line local change inside `readLogSlice` with no schema, no public-API, and no other call-site impact, and the new tests exercise the exact two paths affected. CI in this repo runs the full lane.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** fast file growth between polls → no rotation message; real rotation (file shrunk) → rotation message still emitted.
- **Edge cases checked:** initial poll with no cursor still uses `start = max(0, size - maxBytes)` and reports `truncated` only (unchanged); empty file still returns `cursor: size, lines: []` (unchanged); cursor inside `[start, size]` window with `size - start <= maxBytes` is unchanged.
- **What I did NOT verify:** live CLI output against a running daemon during a real burst (will rely on CI + reviewer spot-check).

- **Risk:** a downstream consumer relies on `reset === true` to mean "skipped some lines" rather than "the cursor is no longer meaningful".
  - **Mitigation:** I grepped the repo for `payload.reset` / `LogTailPayload`. The only consumers are `src/cli/logs-cli.ts` (renders the user-visible string this PR is correcting) and `src/logging/diagnostic-support-export.ts::sanitizeLogTail` (passes the boolean through verbatim into the support bundle). Both are display-only; neither uses `reset` to decide whether lines were skipped — they use the existing `truncated` flag for that.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `readLogSlice` in `src/logging/log-tail.ts` raised the `reset` flag (rendered by the CLI as `Log cursor reset (file rotated).`) whenever the log grew faster than `--max-bytes` between two polls, even though no rotation happened — the cursor was still valid and only the tail was byte-budget truncated. Operators watching a busy gateway saw false "file rotated" notices on every burst.

**Real environment tested:** Local Node v22 runtime (Ubuntu 24.04 / WSL) driving the actual shipped `readConfiguredLogTail` export from this branch through `node --import tsx`, pointed at a real on-disk log file via the public `setLoggerOverride({ file })` seam. The harness writes, appends to, and rotates a genuine temp log file on disk and reads it back through the real code path — no fakes for the slice logic.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
node --import tsx ./proof_logtail.ts
// proof_logtail.ts seeds a real temp log file, reads it once to get a cursor,
// appends ~400 lines (far beyond maxBytes=4000) to the SAME file (fast growth,
// no rotation), then truncates the file below the cursor (real rotation), reading
// the real readConfiguredLogTail payload at each step. Run against upstream/main and this branch.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Real console output captured from the command above, run first against the current upstream/main slice logic, then against this branch:
```
########## BEFORE PATCH (current upstream/main log-tail.ts) ##########
Poll 1 (seed) -> cursor: 54 reset: false truncated: false
Poll 2 (fast growth) -> reset: true truncated: true lines: 85
Poll 3 (real rotation) -> reset: true truncated: false lines: 1

########## AFTER PATCH (this branch log-tail.ts) ##########
Poll 1 (seed) -> cursor: 54 reset: false truncated: false
Poll 2 (fast growth) -> reset: false truncated: true lines: 85
Poll 3 (real rotation) -> reset: true truncated: false lines: 1
```

**Observed result after fix:** Before the change, Poll 2 (fast growth, same file) reported `reset: true` — a false rotation signal. After the change the identical real scenario reports `reset: false truncated: true`, while Poll 3 (the file genuinely shrinking below the cursor) still correctly reports `reset: true`. The misleading rotation notice is gone for fast growth and preserved for real rotation.

**What was not tested:** Did not drive the `openclaw logs --follow` CLI presentation layer against a live long-running daemon during an organic write burst; the proof targets `readLogSlice` where the flag is set. The CLI string mapping of `reset` is unchanged by this PR.

target.title: fix(googlechat): preserve thread reply target through delivery
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** Google Chat replies could leave the inbound Chat thread and appear as new top-level messages. - **Why it matters:** delayed or tool-driven assistant replies break group-thread UX when they detach from the conversation the user spoke into. - **What changed:** preserve `message.thread.name` as `MessageThreadId`, expose it to reply/message-tool threading, and normalize Google Chat outbound thread targets before they reach `thread.name`. - **What did NOT change:** no Google Chat per-thread OpenClaw session isolation; core reply plumbing remains channel-agnostic and Google Chat resource-name handling stays behind the Google Chat boundary. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #69422 - Related #39554 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Google Chat assistant replies from existing Chat threads should remain in the inbound thread. - Real environment tested: Linux VPS-hosted OpenClaw Google Chat runtime with service-account auth and a real Google Chat space. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Jai sent real Google Chat threaded messages and confirmed assistant replies stayed in-thread before this PR was marked ready. - Evidence after fix: screenshot evidence is attached below: - Before: Google Chat reply appeared outside the inbound thread. - After: Google Chat reply stayed inside the inbound thread. - Scope note: typing-placeholder editing is separate follow-up behavior; this PR fixes thread placement. - Observed result after fix: live Google Chat replies stayed in-thread; local seam tests also verified natural replies, message-tool sends, and current-reply behavior use the inbound Google Chat thread resource. - What was not tested: every Google Chat account/space policy combination; typing-placeholder edit/cleanup for message-tool sends; Google Chat per-thread OpenClaw session isolation, which is out of scope. - Before evidence: prior behavior re-rooted replies as new top-level Chat messages. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: Google Chat exposes different resource shapes for messages (`spaces/.../messages/...`) and threads (`spaces/.../threads/...`), and OpenClaw previously let message-shaped values flow into thread-routing seams. - Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered legacy single-resource channels, but not a provider where current message id and current thread id are different resource names. - Contributing context: - Inbound webhook context did not populate generic `MessageThreadId` from `message.thread.name`. - The message-tool path had no inbound-thread default when the agent omitted `threadId`. - `[[reply_to_current]]` resolves to the current message id; for Google Chat that value must be converted to the inbound thread before delivery. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-plumbing.test.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.webhook-routing.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.reply-delivery.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts` - `extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: Google Chat has a current message resource and a distinct current thread resource; implicit replies and message-tool sends use the thread resource, while message-shaped reply ids are rewritten or rejected before reaching the Chat API. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers webhook ingress, core reply seams, message-tool routing, channel delivery, and the API boundary without requiring live Google Chat in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): none. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Google Chat assistant replies from an existing Chat thread now stay in that thread instead of creating a new top-level message. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: Google Chat webhook -> currentMessageId=spaces/.../messages/... -> delivery sends that as thread.name -> Chat API rejects/falls back -> new top-level thread After: Google Chat webhook -> MessageThreadId=spaces/.../threads/... -> reply/tool delivery resolves outbound thread -> Chat API receives thread.name=spaces/.../threads/... ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS local checkout; Linux VPS-hosted runtime for real Chat verification - Runtime/container: OpenClaw source checkout; local verification on exact rebased head `a617644c81dd43bb7285d3001ca6d19750e62e68`; live Google Chat verification on a Linux VPS-hosted runtime with the thread-placement patch applied - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat service-account auth - Relevant config (redacted): Google Chat enabled with group mention policy; no secret/config changes required ### Steps 1. Send a threaded Google Chat message that mentions the bot. 2. Let the assistant produce a normal final reply. 3. Exercise message-tool sends without explicit `threadId`. 4. Exercise `[[reply_to_current]]` behavior where the core seam supplies the current message id. ### Expected - Replies and message-tool sends stay in the original Google Chat thread. - Explicit thread-shaped ids are preserved. - Message-shaped or malformed thread targets never reach `thread.name` as-is. ### Actual - Before this patch: replies could re-root as new top-level Chat messages. - After this patch: live verification and local seam coverage keep replies in the inbound thread. - Scope note: typing-placeholder editing is separate follow-up behavior and is not fixed by this PR. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Screenshot evidence attached below: - Before screenshot: Google Chat reply escaped the inbound thread and appeared outside the original conversation thread. - After screenshot: Google Chat reply stayed inside the inbound thread. - Scope note: the after screenshot may still show a stale typing placeholder when the message-tool path is used; typing-placeholder edit/cleanup is not part of this PR. Verification commands run on exact rebased head `a617644c81dd43bb7285d3001ca6d19750e62e68` after the May 26 rebase: ```bash mise exec node@22.22.2 -- node scripts/docs-list.js # passed mise exec node@22.22.2 -- ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/actions.ts extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts extensions/googlechat/src/channel.adapters.ts extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/google-auth.runtime.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/google-auth.runtime.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor-reply-delivery.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.reply-delivery.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.webhook-routing.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/thread-resource.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-payloads-base.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-plumbing.test.ts # All matched files use the correct format. mise exec node@22.22.2 -- node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/google-auth.runtime.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.reply-delivery.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.webhook-routing.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-payloads.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-plumbing.test.ts # 2 Vitest shards passed; 330 tests passed mise exec node@22.22.2 -- node scripts/changed-lanes.mjs --json # lanes=core, coreTests, extensions, extensionTests, docs ``` Additional broad test signal from pre-ready validation on a prior PR head: ```bash pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply extensions/googlechat extensions/telegram # auto-reply: 113 files, 1508 tests passed # telegram: 106 files, 1483 tests passed # googlechat shard was rerun after fixture alignment: 15 files, 141 tests passed ``` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Jai verified live Google Chat thread placement on a real Google Chat runtime and attached before/after screenshots below. - I verified the exact rebased code locally with the commands listed above. - Edge cases checked: - `[[reply_to_current]]` can still resolve to the core current message id; Google Chat rewrites message-shaped values at the channel boundary. - Explicit thread-shaped `replyToId` wins over the inbound thread. - Malformed thread values are rejected before reaching the Google Chat send API. - Message-tool sends without `threadId` use the inbound thread only when the current target matches and reply mode allows implicit threading. - What you did **not** verify: - Every Google Chat account/space policy combination. - Per-thread OpenClaw session isolation for Google Chat; this PR does not implement it. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: callers that accidentally pass a Google Chat message resource as `thread` now fail loudly instead of silently falling back. - Mitigation: channel delivery rewrites known message-resource reply targets to the inbound thread; the API guard reports invalid values before sending. - Risk: a future non-message, non-thread Google Chat resource shape could appear in `replyToId`. - Mitigation: only `spaces/.../threads/...` passes as `thread.name`; everything else is dropped or falls back to the inbound thread. --- ## Screenshots (Bad, Good) ### Bad (before patch) ### Good (after patch)
